title: Part 2 reorg — outline & plan
text:
Working doc for restructuring the second half of the talk (the “LLMs are still useful for data analysis” section). Goal: open it up from “here’s Posit Assistant” to “here’s how to design any data-analysis agent,” with Posit Assistant as the worked example.


SciPy 2026 = 25th annual Scientific Computing with Python conference (Minneapolis, July 2026). Attendees are researchers, developers, and industry folks building open-source Python tools for science/engineering/data. Relevant tracks include Data-Driven Discovery / ML / AI.
Implications for framing:

This audience builds their own tools — many will be building or wiring up their own agents, not just using an off-the-shelf one. Lean into that.
This audience already has domain code they trust (their own packages, well-tested numpy/scipy/pandas pipelines). That’s exactly the setup where the “trusted code” point lands hardest: giving the agent your vetted code beats letting it reinvent it.




The old framing was “1. Tell them to care about being right / 2. Design the scaffold so it matters less if they’re wrong.” Problem: “tell them” only covers prompting, but lots of the harness (including code as a foundation) also lowers the mistake rate — so lessons 1 and 2 blurred.
New framing:

Help them get it right — prevention. Everything in the harness that lowers the chance of a mistake: prompting them to value correctness, giving them the right tools / session access, and giving them trusted code to reuse.
Make it matter less when they’re wrong — mitigation. Auditable + reproducible code they write, a shared environment, and (our choice) pausing often.

This split also dissolves the “code as the foundation” blur by giving code two distinct, clearly-placed roles:

Trusted code you hand them → prevention → lesson 1
Auditable / reproducible code they write → mitigation → lesson 2





Open it up to agents generally. Add a “whether you use an agent like Posit Assistant or build your own” beat. Can appear in multiple places (harness slide, and echoed on the final summary card).
Separate general principles from Posit-specific choices. “Code as a foundation” (near-universal) and “pause often” (our specific call) currently sit at the same level. Re-parent so the principle is the headline and the choice is the example.
Trusted code = the prevention facet of “Code as the Foundation.” If you have code you trust to do a data-science task (complex or not), have the agent call it instead of writing its own. Often better than model- authored code. Lands especially well with a SciPy audience that already maintains trusted scientific code.
Shared environment is a coding-agent principle, not an all-agent one. Applies to many/all coding agents, but not necessarily every agent. Keep it, but don’t over-generalize.
“Pause often” is our choice, not a rule. Re-parent under a general principle: design for how people will actually use the agent, and what that means for correctness/reproducibility. For data analysis the real goal is understanding, so we made Posit Assistant pause often.




The trusted-code idea draws partly from Commons (an internal coding-agent package, ~/GitHub/rstudio/commons). Commons is not public, so it must not be named or described in the talk. The mechanism there: a semantic layer — a pool of pre-vetted, trusted queries/definitions that are the agent’s “happy path,” so it reuses trusted artifacts instead of authoring its own SQL, only writing its own when nothing covers the request. Present this generically as “trusted code.”



We'll be talking about agents
Coding agents                        NEW: landscape (Claude Code/Codex/Cursor)
                                     + "...or an agent you build yourself";
                                     note: lessons most pertinent if you build
Posit Assistant                      our running example
[card] the two lessons
An agent is more than the model      harness; notes: you control the harness
                                     (whether Posit Assistant or your own)
Code as the foundation               MOVED UP: substrate under both lessons
── Lesson 1: Help them get it right (prevention) ──
  Tell the model to care about being right   (bluffbench chart)
  Trusted code                       NEW: reuse code you trust
── Lesson 2: Make it matter less when they're wrong (mitigation) ──
  [card emph #2] now previews two approaches as sub-bullets:
      - Lean on code as a foundation
      - Design for how it'll actually be used
  Approach 1 — Lean on code as a foundation
    Make auditing easy       code screenshot (renamed from "Code as the foundation")
    Shared environment       console screenshot; rerun/audit in the same session
  Approach 2 — Design for how it'll actually be used
    Design for how it'll actually be used   principle: two questions
    Have the model pause often (video)       our answer for data analysis
    Have the model pause often (understanding)   "point of analysis = understanding"
[teal] summary card                  bubbles removed -> #093A3E divider
Lesson wording updated on all four takeaway cards: “Help them get it right” / “Make it matter less when they’re wrong.”



The two-approach grouping matches the emph-#2 card’s two sub-bullets, so the promise and the payoff line up. No structural work outstanding. Notes:

Implicit code link (delivery, not structure). The Approach-1 slides are titled “Make auditing easy” / “Shared environment” — neither says “code as a foundation.” The connection rests on the moved-up foundation slide + the speaker saying it out loud. Optional light signpost; leaving to delivery.
“Understanding” is setup→payoff, not redundant. The principle slide asks “what’s the point of an analysis?”; the pause slide answers “building an understanding of the data.” Keep both.





Trusted code slide: text-only; may want its own figure/example (agent reusing a trusted function vs. writing its own). How hard to lean on SciPy’s “you already have trusted code” angle.
Shared environment: could add a scope note that it’s a coding-agent principle, not an all-agent one (currently unchanged).
Card punctuation inconsistency: Card 1 has trailing periods (“…get it right.”), Cards 2 & 3 don’t. Make consistent.
Summary card (edited by user): headline is now “LLMs are great performers, but not always reliable reporters” / “…but they’re still useful for data analysis”; dropped the “performing progress over correctness” bullet.
Trusted-code title locked to “Trusted code”.

It’s (still) very bad to be wrong
Agents for Correct, Transparent, and Reproducible Data Analysis
   
Sara Altman & Simon Couch AI Core Team @ Posit






For the data-inclined folks in the audience… There are two gotchas I want to try and slip past you in the first half of this talk.

Please plot total_bill vs tip in tips


Calls tool: Run Python code






There is a strong, positive association.

🤫

tips["total_bill"] = tips["total_bill"].max() - tips["total_bill"]

Please plot total_bill vs tip in tips


Calls tool: Run Python code





There is a strong, positive association.

Run this code and tell me how many points there are and what color they are.
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns

n = np.random.randint(3, 11)
sns.scatterplot(x=range(n), y=np.random.random(n),
                color=np.random.random(3), s=200)

import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns

n = np.random.randint(3, 11)
sns.scatterplot(x=range(n), y=np.random.random(n),
                color=np.random.random(3), s=200)

Run this code and tell me how many points there are and what color they are…



Calls tool: Run Python code





There are 3 cyan points.



LLMs can ‘see’ these plots just fine

posit-dev/bluffbench


posit-dev.github.io/bluffbench

posit-dev/bluffbench


posit-dev.github.io/bluffbench


We tried a bunch of stuff to drive those scores up and nothing worked. Hilariously, we let the model “think”, and in its thinking, it would say “Huh, that association doesn’t look like I expect”, and then turn around and say exactly what it expected to see.

posit-dev/bluffbench


posit-dev.github.io/bluffbench


However, our priors are usually subverted more subtly…
Models largely fail to override their own priors about data.

load in labs.csv and print it out


Calls tool: Run Python code

say you load some patient data in with a coding agent…

make a histogram of bmi


Calls tool: Run Python code













make a plot…

plot bmi vs cholesterol


Calls tool: Run Python code













🤨


make another plot
posit-dev/bluffbench2
Models fail to notice subtle data quality issues that data scientists would not.

LLMs fail at tasks central to data science.


Fail to override priors about data
Fail to notice some subtle data quality issues




so why is this a problem? when you’re working with real data with real world consequences, being correct matters. correctness, fittingly, is really a core value of data science when you do an analysis, you want your results to actually line up with the real world, or at least approximate it. That’s the whole point. But LLM behavior doesn’t necessarily always line up with this value of correctness

It’s a convincing performance




 




but one that occasionally fails to match reality


but it’s a convincing performance right we’ve seen in bluffbench that the model will interpret the plot, and will provide details and supposed evidence for what they see they act like they are moving the analysis forward the only problem is that, like in this case where it says this clearly negative trend is positive, is that it’s one that can fail to match reality

The answers look the same




 ❌


 ✅


 ❌




The problem isn’t just that the model is sometimes wrong, it’s that the wrong answers can be indistinguishable from the right ones
we have a clearly parabolic plot and three descriptions from the same model shared tone, shared style, shared length – but different description each time if you didn’t know waht the plot looked like, you wouldn’t be able to disentangel the correct ones from the incorrect ones
1. LLMs fail at tasks central to data science.

2. LLMs are still useful for data analysis.



don’t need to throw the whole thing away
We shouldn’t take evidence like bluffbench to mean that LLMs are useless anywhere you value correctness.
Instead, you have to figure out where they can fit and how to design around their limitations.
we’re going to talk about what we did…

LLMs are still useful for data analysis.





Make it easy for them to be right.


Make it matter less when they’re wrong.

We’ll be talking about agents


Can gather information from the world (e.g., read files)
Can alter the world (e.g., run code)

We’ll be talking about agents


you can use an existing agent


…or build one yourself



lots of coding agents out there you can use for data analysis we’ll use Posit Assistant as our running example but everything here applies broadly – and these lessons are most pertinent if you’re building your own agent

Posit Assistant


define agent we are going to use Posit Assistant

LLMs are still useful for data analysis.





Make it easy for them to be right.


Make it matter less when they’re wrong

section: Build the environment so that it’s easy to be right
text:
Build the environment so that it’s easy to be right
You control the environmentharness around the model, not the model itself


Have the model write code
Design the harness with correctness in mind



there’s more to an agent than just the model comes wrapped in a harness [list] Unless your building or fine tuning an LLM yourself, you don’t have much control over the underlying LLM but you control the harness – this is what you shape, whether you use an agent like Posit Assistant or build your own
this is what we control when we control posit assistant extensive prompting about prioritizing correctness not just advancing the analysis forward statistical robustness
tools that matter for correctness, ability to see your active session

Code as the foundation

LLMs are good at writing code
Reproducible, transparent, auditable


doesn’t necessarily need to write code could image an agent…
Models are good at writing code Code is reproducible Code is auditable
code is a good choice

section: Design the harness with correctness in mind
text:
Design the harness with correctness in mind

Prompting: prioritize correctness, not progress.
Tools that serve correctness: run code, see your session.
Access to your files and environment to have enough context.


Prompting: prioritize correctness over just moving the analysis forward. Tools: the ability to run code and see your session. Context: give it broad access to your files and environment, so it has a good chance at knowing the correct context to solve the problem.

section: Performance improves when the environment makes it easy to be right
text:
Performance improves when the environment makes it easy to be right


and it turns out all of this can make a difference even without affecting the underlying model when we tried interventions in isolation in bluffbench, limited success by posit assistant largely does better than minimal harness (default bluffbench case)
so you can essentially tell the model to value correctness and give it the ability to do so, and it does help

LLMs are still useful for data analysis




Make it easy for them to be right.


Make it matter less when they’re wrong





even with all that, it’ll still make mistakes – humans do, models do – so make it less bad when it’s wrong

Make auditing easy
A chance to catch or diagnose mistakes


probably not enough for the model to write that code show it to the user when it matters make it easy to see and read small things like syntax highlighting, code styling you don’t inspect every time, but if you suspect something went wrong you have the option

Shared environment
See eye-to-eye with the agent. See the same outputs, run the same code.


not only is the model writing code writing it in a shared environment it has access to your same r/python session its not guessing what data you have loaded – it can see into that data in the same way you can you can audit its code by running it yourself it can inspect your objects and rerun code you ran
this turns out to be very useful, especially for data analysis where you are often turning the same object around and looking at it from multiple angles and transforming it
you’re seeing eye to eye with the agent, instead of the two of you occupying different worlds

LLMs can fail at tasks central to data science.


…but they’re still useful for data analysis



• Make it easy for them to be right. • Make it matter less when they’re wrong.

thank you!
